key_insights.insight: An imbalanced oral microbiome significantly elevates the risk of systemic diseases, including infertility, heart disease, and Alzheimer's.
key_insights.quote: Having an imbalanced oral microbiome increases your risk of oral diseases like gum disease and decay, but also can increase your risk of other systemic diseases like high blood pressure, heart disease, infertility, Alzheimer's.

key_insights.insight: Gum disease is linked to an increased risk of COVID-19 complications, as inflammation in the gums amplifies the body's inflammatory response to the virus.
key_insights.quote: COVID sufferers or patients who had gum disease were nine times more likely to have COVID complications... it's just adding petrol to the fire. So you're just making that cytokine storm even worse.

key_insights.insight: Gum disease can significantly affect fertility, with studies showing that treatment can improve pregnancy rates for men and decrease time to conception for women.
key_insights.quote: Over 90% of men who wasn't able to conceive with their partners had oral disease. But those who got treated, there was a 70% improvement in pregnancy...if a woman has gum disease, it takes them two months longer to conceive.

key_insights.insight: Men with periodontal disease are significantly more likely to experience erectile dysfunction due to impaired blood vessel dilation.
key_insights.quote: Men who have periodontal disease are 2.85 times more likely to suffer from erectile dysfunction.

key_insights.insight: The specific oral bacteria Fusobacterium nucleatum has been linked to more aggressive tumor growth in breast and colorectal cancer, highlighting a potential connection between oral health and cancer progression.
key_insights.quote: Oral bacteria made the cancer more aggressive and harder to treat as well... Fusobacterium nucleatum, which has been shown to accelerate tumor growth within mice, but also for colorectal cancer and breast cancer as well.

key_insights.insight: Maintaining good oral hygiene can help slow cognitive decline in individuals with Alzheimer's disease, as gum disease accelerates the decline.
key_insights.quote: The patients who had gum disease had a much more rapid cognitive decline than those patients who didn't have gum disease...it's still important if you do get Alzheimer's that you maintain your oral health, that you have someone help you brush your teeth because your cognitive decline will be faster.

key_insights.insight: Eating habits influence oral health significantly; frequent sugar consumption, such as sipping sugary tea over a long period, is more detrimental than consuming the same amount of sugar in one go.
key_insights.quote: If you have your hot tea with five lumps of sugar in there, and you're sipping it over an hour or two, that's where you start to see a lot of problems. So actually, you need to be having a sugar attack, so just all the sugar in one go.

key_insights.insight: The mouth offers the first sign that something might be happening from a mental health perspective.
key_insights.quote: It's the first thing that they kind of let go of is their oral health.

actionable_advice.advice: Wait 30 minutes after consuming sugary or acidic foods before brushing your teeth.
actionable_advice.how_to: Chew sugar-free gum or use a neutralizing mint in the interim to reduce the acidity in your mouth.

actionable_advice.advice: Choose a toothpaste that does not contain sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS).
actionable_advice.how_to: Read the ingredient list of your toothpaste and opt for SLS-free options to avoid stripping the inner lining of your mouth.

actionable_advice.advice: Drink sugary or acidic beverages through a straw.
actionable_advice.how_to: Use a straw to bypass the teeth and reduce the contact of these substances with the tooth surface.

actionable_advice.advice: Prioritize using an electric toothbrush and brush for the full 2 minutes.
actionable_advice.how_to: Get an electric toothbrush with timer, paying special attention to the insides of back, bottom teeth, brushing gums with circular movements.

memorable_stories: The dentist had a patient with severe rheumatoid arthritis whose condition improved dramatically after treatment of her gum disease, enabling her to walk again.
The dentist had a patient who was kissing their dogs who had bad breath. After the dogs were treated, the patient's bad breath went away.
contrarian_claims: Mouthwash, if used regularly without specific prescription, can imbalance the oral microbiome by killing both good and bad bacteria, and should only be used in specific situations or as directed by a dentist.
Consuming sugar all at once (a 'sugar attack') is healthier for your teeth than sipping sugary beverages over extended periods, because this causes a longer disruption of the saliva.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - The Functional Dentist: New Research Linking Bleeding Gums & Cancer! Your Oral Microbiome Can Kill You! If You Can't Get It Up, Brush Your Teeth!
